svg.text: Six Layers of HV Safety in an EV Battery System
Defence-in-depth: each layer stops hazards the layer above missed
Layer 1 — Cell Chemistry Selection
LFP cells: olivine structure releases minimal O₂ during thermal runaway → flame-free venting in most cases
NMC: higher energy density but releases O₂ above ~180°C → requires stronger containment in Layers 2–6
Cell-level safety features: safety vent (CID), PTC fuse, separator shutdown (melt at ~130°C)
Layer 2 — Cell-Level Fuses & AFE Hardware Protection
Each cell string has a fuse (pyro-fuse or wire fuse rated 2–5× normal current)
AFE IC hardware comparators: fire CHG/DSG FET off in <1 ms on OV/UV/OT — no MCU latency
Layer 3 — BMS Software Protection (SOC / SOP / fault management)
MCU monitors all cell voltages, currents, temperatures every 10ms — multi-threshold alerts (L1 warn, L2 derate, L3 shutdown)
Opens CHG/DSG contactors on exceeded limits | Logs DTCs to NV memory | Broadcasts fault on CAN bus
Layer 4 — HVIL Loop + Main Contactors
HVIL (High Voltage Interlock Loop): 12V pilot through every HV connector — open connector = pilot lost = contactors open instantly
Main contactor rated for HV arc interruption (DC-rated, not AC) | Pyro-fuse in series as last resort
Layer 5 — Pack Mechanical & Thermal Containment
Firewall / mica sheets between cell groups slow thermal runaway propagation (AIS-156 / ECE R100 requirement)
Orange HV cables (visual identification) | Drain plugs for vented gas | Sealed aluminum enclosure (IP67)
Layer 6 — Vehicle-Level Safety Systems
Crash sensor (accelerometer) triggers pyro-fuse: cuts HV bus within 1ms of impact regardless of BMS state
eCall / automatic hazard lights on thermal runaway DTC | Service mode: orange plug kills HVIL for safe workshop access
Defence-in-depth: a hazard must defeat ALL 6 layers simultaneously — each layer is independently reliable
